**Christian Democratic and Flemish**[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Christen-Democratisch en Vlaams*, pronounced [[ˌkrɪstə(n)deːmoːˈkraːtis ɛɱ ˈvlaːms]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-Christen-Democratisch_en_Vlaams.ogg "File:Nl-Christen-Democratisch en Vlaams.ogg"); **CD&V**) is a Flemish [Christian-democratic](/wiki/Christian-democratic "Christian-democratic") [political party](/wiki/Political_party "Political party") in Belgium.[[2]](#cite_note-Nordsieck-2)[[3]](#cite_note-AnttiroikoMälkiä2007-3) The party has historical ties to both trade unionism ([ACV](/wiki/Confederation_of_Christian_Trade_Unions "Confederation of Christian Trade Unions")) and trade associations ([UNIZO](/wiki/Union_of_Self-Employed_Entrepreneurs "Union of Self-Employed Entrepreneurs")) and the Farmer's League. Until 2001, the party was named the **Christian People's Party** (*Christelijke Volkspartij* [[ˈkrɪstələkə ˈvɔlkspɑrˌtɛi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch"), **CVP**).

It was traditionally the largest political party of Flanders, until it was overtaken by the [New Flemish Alliance](/wiki/New_Flemish_Alliance "New Flemish Alliance") (N-VA) in the 2010s. CD&V participated in most governments and has generally the largest number of mayors. Most [prime ministers of Belgium](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Belgium "Prime Minister of Belgium") and [minister-presidents of Flanders](/wiki/Minister-President_of_Flanders "Minister-President of Flanders") have been CD&V politicians. [Herman Van Rompuy](/wiki/Herman_Van_Rompuy "Herman Van Rompuy"), the [president of the European Council](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Council "President of the European Council") from 2009 to 2014, is one of the leading politicians of CD&V.

CD&V is a member of the [European People's Party](/wiki/European_People%27s_Party "European People's Party") (EPP) and [Centrist Democrat International](/wiki/Centrist_Democrat_International "Centrist Democrat International").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_Democratic_and_Flemish&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The history of the CD&V dates back to the 19th century. It originated in the 19th century [Catholic Party](/wiki/Catholic_Party_(Belgium) "Catholic Party (Belgium)"). At the end of the century, the new fraction of Christian democrats shifted the focus of the party slightly to the left. In the interwar years the party was renamed [Catholic Bloc](/wiki/Catholic_Party_(Belgium) "Catholic Party (Belgium)"). Then, the [Christian Social Party](/wiki/Christian_Social_Party_(Belgium,_defunct) "Christian Social Party (Belgium, defunct)") (PSC-CVP) existed from 1945 until 1968. In 1968, the PSC-CVP was split into the French-speaking Christian Social Party (PSC, now [Les Engagés](/wiki/Les_Engag%C3%A9s "Les Engagés")) and Flemish Christian People's Party (Christelijke Volkspartij CVP).[[11]](#cite_note-Lamberts1997-11)[[12]](#cite_note-Caramani2004-12) In 2001 the CVP changed its name to the CD&V.

The party was almost continually in power from its establishment until 1999, with the exception of 1954–1958. In 1999, the [Flemish Liberals and Democrats](/wiki/Open_Vlaamse_Liberalen_en_Democraten "Open Vlaamse Liberalen en Democraten") (VLD) became the largest party in Belgium, and formed a majority [purple government](/wiki/Purple_(government) "Purple (government)") of liberals, social democrats and greens. The same happened in the Flemish Government, but with the addition of Flemish nationalists. In 2003 CD&V again lost the federal elections which continued the federal centre-left coalition, but this time without the Greens.

In 2004, Flemish elections were held and the CD&V once more became the largest political party by representation in the Flemish Parliament. [Yves Leterme](/wiki/Yves_Leterme "Yves Leterme") became Flemish minister-president. After successful local elections in 2006, the party became the largest party in the Belgian Chamber of Representatives after the [federal elections of 2007](/wiki/2007_Belgian_general_election "2007 Belgian general election"). The CD&V led the subsequent coalition talks, which repeatedly stalled (see [2007–2008 Belgian government formation](/wiki/2007%E2%80%932008_Belgian_government_formation "2007–2008 Belgian government formation")). On the 20 March 2008, a new [federal government](/wiki/Leterme_I_Government "Leterme I Government") was finally assembled, led by Yves Leterme. [Kris Peeters](/wiki/Kris_Peeters "Kris Peeters") subsequently became the next minister-president of Flanders. From 30 December 2008 till 25 November 2009, [Herman Van Rompuy](/wiki/Herman_Van_Rompuy "Herman Van Rompuy") led his first [cabinet](/wiki/Van_Rompuy_I_Government "Van Rompuy I Government") before becoming the first permanent [president of the European Council](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Council "President of the European Council"). Afterwards, Yves Leterme led his [second government](/wiki/Leterme_II_Government "Leterme II Government").

In June 2009, [Flemish elections](/wiki/2009_Belgian_regional_elections "2009 Belgian regional elections") were held and the CD&V remained the largest party of Flanders. [Kris Peeters](/wiki/Kris_Peeters "Kris Peeters") stayed as [Flemish minister-president](/wiki/Minister-President_of_Flanders "Minister-President of Flanders"). The party also remained the largest Flemish party in the European Parliament after the [2009 European elections](/wiki/2009_European_Parliament_election_in_Belgium "2009 European Parliament election in Belgium").

In 2010, the [Open Flemish Liberals and Democrats](/wiki/Open_Vlaamse_Liberalen_en_Democraten "Open Vlaamse Liberalen en Democraten") (Open VLD) decided to step out of the federal coalition, consequently ending the government. The [federal elections of 2010](/wiki/2010_Belgian_general_election "2010 Belgian general election") resulted in a major loss of historic proportions for CD&V, largely due to votes lost to the Flemish-nationalist [New Flemish Alliance](/wiki/New_Flemish_Alliance "New Flemish Alliance"). In the campaign, former prime minister [Yves Leterme](/wiki/Yves_Leterme "Yves Leterme") took a subtle step aside as frontrunner of the party. The president of the party, [Marianne Thyssen](/wiki/Marianne_Thyssen "Marianne Thyssen"), had pre-electorally stated that she was a candidate to become prime minister.

On 6 December 2011, the [Di Rupo Government](/wiki/Di_Rupo_Government "Di Rupo Government") was formed, with the CD&V as the largest Flemish party.

During the [local elections in 2012](/wiki/2012_Belgian_provincial_and_municipal_elections "2012 Belgian provincial and municipal elections"), CD&V managed to remain the largest Flemish party on the local level. It remained in the coalition of all five Flemish provinces and in three quarters of the municipalities. Almost half of these municipalities were subsequently led by CD&V mayors.

## Ideology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_Democratic_and_Flemish&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ideology")]

CD&V is a Flemish party that follows the principle of [Christian democracy](/wiki/Christian_democracy "Christian democracy"). Its core values include the importance of the [family](/wiki/Family "Family"), promoting a respectful and warm society, and recognizing the intrinsic value of each individual. The party places a strong emphasis on [well-being](/wiki/Well-being "Well-being") and [health care](/wiki/Health_care "Health care"), as well as the pursuit of an equitable income for all citizens. However, on the ethical front, CD&V holds conservative positions, particularly on issues such as [abortion](/wiki/Abortion "Abortion") and [euthanasia](/wiki/Euthanasia "Euthanasia"), where the party is reluctant to fast-track relaxations. Combining these conservative ethics with progressive socioeconomic policies, CD&V is often considered a "centre party". In its administrative activities, CD&V nurtures close ties with civil society, including [trade unions](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union"), professional federations and various associations. In doing so, the party strives to represent the voice of [rural areas](/wiki/Rural "Rural") and the [agricultural sector](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture").[[13]](#cite_note-vrt20240322-13)
